result:
The rain came down in that lazy, persistent way it does in late October, not quite a storm but enough to make the whole world feel like it was exhaling. I stood at the window of the old farmhouse I’d rented for no particularly good reason, watching water bead and race down the glass. The fields outside had turned the color of wet cardboard. Somewhere beyond them, a line of birches shivered like they were cold and embarrassed about it.

I’ve been thinking a lot lately about how we romanticize solitude until we actually have it. Then it stops being a soft-focus Instagram aesthetic and starts being a presence in the room that doesn’t pay rent and never does the dishes. Solitude has a smell. It smells like coffee left on the warmer too long and the particular metallic tang of thoughts that have been recirculated too many times.

This house creaks in ways that feel intentional. Not the random settling of old wood, but sentences. The stairs say something different every time I go up them. The attic door sighs like it’s disappointed in me. Last night the wind found a loose shutter and spent twenty minutes clapping it like slow, sarcastic applause. I clapped back. We reached an understanding.

I’ve been writing again, which is dangerous. Not the act itself—though Lord knows that has its hazards—but the permission I gave myself to do it without purpose. No pitch. No audience in mind. Just the pure decadent indulgence of putting one word after another because the sound of them pleases me. It feels vaguely criminal, like eating dessert before dinner or calling in sick to watch old French films with the subtitles off just to see if I can still follow the story with my terrible accent and worse memory.

The thing nobody tells you about getting older is how interesting your own mind becomes once you stop trying to impress it. For decades I treated my thoughts like an unruly employee who needed constant performance reviews. Now I mostly just follow them around with a notebook like some slightly bewildered naturalist. *Oh look, it’s doing the thing again where it connects a childhood memory of my grandmother’s hands to the smell of diesel fuel on a cold morning in 2026. How strange. How lovely.*

There’s a spider living in the corner of the bathroom window. I’ve named her Deborah. She’s built something architectural and ambitious and completely impractical, the kind of web that makes you wonder if she’s read too much Ayn Rand. Every morning she repairs the damage I do when I stumble in half-blind for water. We have developed what I can only describe as a tense but functional co-parenting relationship regarding the flies.

I’ve been reading poetry again too. Not the respectable kind. The weird stuff. The kind that feels like it was written by someone having a conversation with God while simultaneously trying to hotwire a car. There’s a line from a poet whose name I’ve already forgotten that goes: “I have worn the same face for so long even the mirror has started lying out of politeness.” I keep it in my pocket like a smooth stone. Take it out and turn it over when nobody’s looking.

The world feels both too big and too small these days. We carry the entire screaming nervous system of humanity in our pockets and then wonder why we can’t sleep. Meanwhile the mushrooms keep doing their quiet, patient work beneath the forest floor, connecting trees in languages we’re too loud to hear. Sometimes I go stand among the birches at dusk and try to apologize to them with my mind. I don’t know if it works. They never write back.

I made bread yesterday. Not because I needed bread but because I wanted to participate in something ancient and stupid and beautiful. The dough rose like it was showing off. I talked to it while I kneaded. Told it my problems. It didn’t offer advice, which I appreciated. When it came out of the oven it looked like a small golden continent. I burned my fingers breaking off a piece and ate it standing up at the counter like an animal. It tasted like forgiveness.

There’s something profound about the way light moves through this house in the afternoon. It finds all the places I’ve been avoiding. The dust on top of the bookshelf. The photograph of my father I still haven’t hung. The place where the floorboards don’t match because someone, decades ago, got ambitious and then gave up halfway through. The light doesn’t judge. It just says: *Here is what exists. Look at it if you want.*

I’ve been practicing wanting less. Not in the tidy minimalist sense that looks good on a vision board. More like a geological process. Slow erosion of the part of me that thought happiness was something I could achieve if I just assembled the right collection of experiences and objects and versions of myself. The wanting still exists, but it’s changed shape. It feels more like being in love with the world in spite of all evidence to the contrary. A soft, stubborn kind of devotion.

Last night I dreamed I was a river. Not floating in one—actually being the river. The dream didn’t come with any helpful symbolism or neat psychological insight. It was just wet and loud and full of leaves and small fish and the particular melancholy that belongs to anything that only knows how to move in one direction. I woke up thirsty in a way that felt cosmic.

The rain has stopped now. The clouds are doing that thing where they tear themselves open just enough to let through a shaft of light that looks like God changing His mind. The birches have stopped trembling. Even Deborah seems to be taking a break from her Sisyphean web maintenance.

I think I’ll go for a walk.

There’s a dirt road that leads into the woods behind the house. I don’t know where it ends and I like it that way. The kind of path that asks you questions instead of answering them. My boots are by the door, crusted with yesterday’s mud. They look like they’ve been somewhere important without me.

Maybe that’s the secret. Not to arrive anywhere in particular. Just to keep being the kind of person who puts on muddy boots and walks into the woods because the light looked interesting and the silence felt like it might have something to say if you stopped trying to transcribe it.

The house will still be creaking when I get back. Deborah will still be building her ridiculous cathedral. The bread I made yesterday will still be on the counter, slightly stale now, but honest.

And I will still be exactly this: a person-shaped collection of questions and contradictions and the stubborn refusal to stop being moved by ordinary things. A creature who claps back at shutters and talks to dough and keeps a dead poet’s sentence in his pocket like a talisman.

It’s not much.

But it’s mine.

And for once, that feels like enough.

(Word count: 1,012)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
